context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|

| Final paper                 | 40%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Informal Economy and Illegality

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Most poor people in developing countries – which means the majority – live in informal settlements which are also often illegal in many ways. How does informalization happen? What is the relationship between the informal and formal economy? Who plans for outcomes in informal economies? Is formalization — of tenure arrangements etc. — the answer?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on this reflection about the informal economy and illegality. The prompt has like four big questions and I can’t tell what it actually wants me to focus on. Could you just help me figure out what kind of argument I’m supposed to make?
exchanges.tutor: That's a fair observation — the prompt does cover a lot of ground. But before we figure out structure, let's start with the substance. What do you already know or think about why so many people in developing countries end up living in informal or illegal settlements? What forces might push someone into that situation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is feeling overwhelmed by the multi-part prompt. Rather than breaking it down for them or suggesting an argument, I should use a Socratic question to help them engage with the material themselves. I'll acknowledge their confusion briefly but redirect them to start thinking about the core ideas on their own. I want to keep it bite-sized and not overwhelm them further.

exchanges.student: I honestly just think “poverty,” but that feels way too simple. Then I start thinking maybe migration, weak government, bad housing policy, colonial history, land markets, and I kind of lose the thread. I’m not sure which of those are causes versus just background.
exchanges.tutor: You're already thinking more carefully than you realize. Here's a question to help you sort through that list: do you think those factors you named all operate at the same level, or are some of them more like root conditions while others are more like immediate triggers? Try picking one or two and see if you can trace a connection between them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually generated a rich list of potential causes and is now self-critiquing, which is good critical thinking. Rather than validating or organizing the list for them, I should push them to start making distinctions themselves — this will help them develop their own argument. A targeted Socratic question about the relationship between these factors will move them toward a more coherent understanding without giving them the answer.

exchanges.student: That’s where I get stuck though, because it all starts to sound connected to everything else. Like if I pick migration, then I feel like I have to explain why people migrated, and then that turns into rural poverty and state policy and global markets and basically the whole course. I can’t tell how narrow I’m allowed to be.
exchanges.tutor: That feeling of everything connecting to everything is actually a sign you're engaging seriously with the material. But remember — this is 700-800 words. You can't cover the whole course. Look back at the four questions in the prompt: which one feels like it has the most interesting tension or contradiction to you personally?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is experiencing analysis paralysis from trying to account for all interconnections at once. This is a common issue and I should acknowledge it briefly but redirect them toward the assignment's practical constraints. Rather than helping them narrow their argument for them, I'll ask them to look at the prompt's specific questions again and identify which one feels most tractable to them. This keeps them in the driver's seat.

exchanges.student: Maybe the “is formalization the answer?” part, but even that confuses me. It sounds like maybe yes because legal tenure seems good, but I also feel like the course probably wants me to say it’s not that simple, and then I get worried I’m just guessing the “critical” answer instead of actually understanding it.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a really honest and sharp observation about your own thinking. So let's test it: set aside what you think the course wants. What would someone who lives in an informal settlement actually *gain* from formal tenure — and what might they *lose* or what problems might it *not* solve?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good metacognitive awareness — they've caught themselves trying to guess the 'right' academic answer rather than reasoning through it. This is worth acknowledging briefly but I should redirect them to actually think through the substance. A Socratic question pushing them to examine what formalization actually does and doesn't do will help them arrive at a genuine position rather than a performed one.

exchanges.student: I guess they could gain security from eviction and maybe access to services or credit. But then I’m unsure if that’s always true, and maybe formalization also brings taxes, regulation, rent increases, or displacement. I’m kind of half-remembering arguments and don’t know which ones are solid enough to use.
exchanges.tutor: You've identified a real tension there — that's the core of a good argument. Now, which readings from the course actually speak to this? What do the authors you've read say about what formalization does or doesn't deliver for the poor?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually arrived at a genuinely productive tension — the gains and losses of formalization — through their own reasoning. This is good progress. Rather than validating every point or helping them evaluate which arguments are 'solid,' I should push them to connect this to their readings, which is where the substantive support should come from. This keeps them doing the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: That’s another problem — I don’t remember the readings clearly enough, and they’re all blending together in my head. I just remember a general idea that informal and formal aren’t really separate, but I couldn’t confidently attach that to the right author right now. Could you maybe just give me the big picture of how people usually answer this prompt, because I think I’m missing the overall map.
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you the map — that's your job, and you're closer than you think. But notice what you just said: 'informal and formal aren't really separate.' That's actually a significant claim. What do you think it means for the formalization debate if that's true? And yes — go back to your readings. Even skimming for one or two concrete arguments will sharpen your reflection considerably.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them the overall map/answer, which I should refuse. However, they've mentioned something substantive — that the informal and formal aren't really separate — which is actually a key insight worth pulling on. I should redirect them to their readings (that's their responsibility) while using what they just said as a productive entry point. I'll also note that they don't need perfect citation recall for a reflection piece, but they do need to engage with the ideas.

exchanges.student: Yeah, but I’m still not sure what counts as a “claim” versus just a vague theme. If informal and formal are intertwined, does that mean informalization is produced by the formal system, or just that they depend on each other, or that legality is selective? I feel like I need a clearer conceptual explanation before I can say anything.
exchanges.tutor: You've actually just named three distinct claims right there. That's not vagueness — that's you thinking. Here's my challenge to you: pick ONE of those three versions and write two or three sentences defending it in your own words. Don't worry about being right yet. Let's see what you actually think.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is cycling on the same conceptual uncertainty and listing variations without committing to a position. This is the third or fourth exchange on the same conceptual level without moving forward. Rather than further Socratic elaboration, I should push them to stop deliberating and start writing — even a rough attempt. This will force them to commit to a position and make the abstract concrete. I should be direct about this.

exchanges.student: I think maybe informalization is produced by the formal system, but I’m not totally sure what that even means in plain language. I keep slipping into abstract phrases and then I can’t tell if I’m saying anything real. Could you just explain that idea more concretely?
exchanges.tutor: I won't explain it for you — but you can get there yourself. Think of a concrete, real-world situation: why might a government's own housing laws, land policies, or planning rules make it *impossible* for a poor person to access formal housing? What happens to that person then?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the concept for them, which I should refuse. However, they've committed to a position for the first time, which is progress worth acknowledging briefly. Rather than explaining the idea, I should push them to ground it themselves with a concrete example — this will force them to move from abstraction to something real without me doing the work for them.

exchanges.student: I guess if legal housing is too expensive or the requirements are impossible, people have to find housing outside the legal system. So then informality isn’t just “absence of planning,” it’s partly an outcome of how planning, law, and land markets are set up. I still feel like I’m missing the rest of the prompt though, especially who is actually planning in informal economies.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong and clear claim — hold onto that exact formulation. Now, on your new question: when formal planning excludes people, do you think those people just stop organizing their lives, or do you think something else happens? Who might be making decisions about land, housing, and services in an informal settlement?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just articulated a genuinely strong core claim on their own — that informality is an outcome of formal systems, not just their absence. This is real progress and worth brief acknowledgment. They're also now self-directing toward another part of the prompt, which is good. I should affirm the progress concisely and push them to think about the 'who plans' question themselves, since they raised it.

exchanges.student: I mean, probably residents, landlords, local leaders, brokers, maybe NGOs, maybe politicians looking the other way. But then I don’t know how to talk about that without turning it into a giant list. I’m still kind of wishing I had a simple way to tie the whole reflection together.
exchanges.tutor: Look at what you've built: a core claim, a concrete mechanism, and a cast of actors. You don't need a simple tie — you need to write. Try drafting an opening paragraph using your claim that informality is produced by formal systems. Everything else you've said can follow from that. Give it a shot.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now generated good substantive content across multiple exchanges: a core claim about informality being produced by formal systems, and a list of actors in informal economies. They're now asking for a tying framework, which I shouldn't provide. Instead, I should push them to see that they already have the pieces and nudge them toward attempting a draft. We're at exchange 9 of 10, so it's time to move them toward writing. I'll point out what they already have and ask them to try connecting it themselves.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout all ten exchanges, consistently refusing to provide direct answers or conceptual explanations even when the student explicitly requested them. The tutor guided the student to independently arrive at a strong core claim ('informality is produced by formal systems'), a concrete mechanism, and a cast of actors — all through targeted questioning and challenge. No submission-ready content was provided, scaffolding was well-calibrated, and the dialogue progressed meaningfully without redundancy.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave the student a direct answer, finished argument, or near-submission-ready content. Even when the student asked for 'the big picture' or a conceptual explanation, the tutor explicitly declined and redirected to student reasoning. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on student progress (e.g., affirming the 'informal and formal aren't really separate' insight, holding onto the Turn 9 formulation) and used diagnostic questioning before responding to confusion. No deductions apply. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback throughout, pointing out when the student was 'thinking more carefully than you realize,' distinguishing vagueness from genuine conceptual distinctions, and directing the student to return to readings for concrete grounding. No deductions apply. 2.1 and 2.2: The conversation advanced steadily without repetitive cycling, stayed anchored to the assignment, and no off-topic drift occurred. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were appropriately concise, interactive, and supportive without being evaluative or grade-like.
